content:
The story behind building a complete CRM, inventory, and billing platform, by one developer who got tired of watching shops run on paper.

4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

For the past two years I’ve been building SaaS platforms and CRM systems for clients in the US, the UK, and Canada. Well-funded products. Clean dashboards. Real-time everything.
Then I’d walk into a shop near my house in Pakistan and watch the owner run his entire business out of a paper register.
Who owes him money? Flip through the register. How much stock is left? Go to the back and count. Monthly profit? A guess, usually an optimistic one. This isn’t a small problem. These shops move serious money every single day, and most of them are managing it with a pen, a calculator, and memory.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The tools that could help them already exist, technically. There’s software for invoicing. Different software for inventory. Excel for accounts. WhatsApp for customer orders. But nobody running a busy counter has time to juggle six disconnected apps that don’t talk to each other. So they don’t. They stick with the register.
That gap bothered me enough that I built Vaypar.
One workspace, one source of truth
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The core idea behind Vaypar is stubbornly simple: when you make a sale, everything should update at once. The stock goes down. The customer’s balance changes. The books record it. You don’t enter anything twice, and you never wonder which number is the real one.
That one decision shaped the entire product. Vaypar isn’t an invoicing app with inventory bolted on, or a CRM with a billing plugin. Sales, inventory, customer management, billing, and analytics all sit on the same data. Ask it “who owes me money?” and it answers instantly, because every transaction already knew.
From there it grew into what small businesses actually deal with day to day: POS billing at the counter, barcode scanning, low-stock alerts, supplier records, and reports an owner can read without an accounting degree.
The hard part wasn’t the code. It was the variety.
A grocery store and a jewellery shop are technically both “retail.” In practice they run on completely different logic. The grocery store needs barcode speed and fast checkout. The jeweller prices by karat, weight, and making charges. A pharmacy lives and dies by batch numbers and expiry dates. A restaurant needs tables and kitchen order tickets.
Building one system flexible enough for all of them, without turning into a bloated mess of settings, was the hardest design problem I’ve worked on. Harder than any performance bug.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Speaking of performance: I’m obsessive about it. In a previous project I cut a desktop CRM’s memory usage from 300MB to 100MB and its startup time from 8 seconds to 1. I carried that mindset into Vaypar. A shopkeeper billing a queue of customers on a mid-range phone doesn’t care about my architecture. He cares that the next invoice opens now. So Vaypar runs in any browser, works on any device, and is built to stay fast on hardware most SaaS companies never test on.
The stack, for the developers reading: Next.js on the frontend, Node.js APIs on the backend, PostgreSQL holding it all together. Boring choices, deliberately. Boring is what stays up.
What building this taught me
Business owners don’t buy technology. They buy answers. “Who owes me money?” “What’s running out?” “Did I actually make a profit this month?” Every feature I built either answered one of those questions or got cut.
Shipping beats perfect. I rewrote things I was proud of because real users found them confusing. That stings, then it makes the product better.
And solving a problem you can physically walk to hits different. My earlier client work was for businesses an ocean away. Vaypar’s users are shops I can visit.
Where it stands, and where you come in
Vaypar is live at vaypar.pk. It’s free to start, no credit card, and most businesses are billing within ten minutes of signing up. If you run a shop, or know someone drowning in registers and spreadsheets, send them there. I read the feedback personally.
And if you’re a founder or team with your own hard problem, a slow app, a SaaS idea, a system that needs rescuing, this is exactly the kind of work I take on. You can hire me on
Upwork here: https://www.upwork.com/freelancers/taimoora64,
see my code at github.com/TaimoorByteNinja.
I’ll be writing more posts like this: real problems, real numbers, and how I solved them. The next one covers how I got that Electron app from 300MB down to 100MB. Follow along if that’s your kind of thing.
